agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to replace 1965 Zoning Bylaw with new 2026 version
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to repeal Burnaby's current Zoning Bylaw (from 1965) and replace it with a new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026. This proposed change would also introduce new Development Permit Area Guidelines and reassign some properties to different zoning districts. If approved, this would update the rules for what you can build and do on your property. The decision has not been made yet — this is an upcoming item on the council agenda.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to speak up about how the new zoning rules would affect your property.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.1
BURNABY ZONING BYLAW - PROPOSED REPEAL AND REPLACEMENT
Purpose: To seek Council approval to repeal Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 and replace it with the proposed Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, including new Development Permit Area Guidelines and zoning district reassignment.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Housing Authority Year 2 operating funding request
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving the Burnaby Housing Authority's request for Year 2 operating funding. This is a proposal to provide money to help the Authority run its housing programs for another year. The decision has not been made yet — Council will vote on whether to approve the funding at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.2
BHA - ASSISTANCE REQUEST FOR YEAR 2 OPERATING FUNDING
Purpose: To seek Council approval of the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation’s assistance request for Year 2 Operating Funding.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 2026 property tax rates for Burnaby businesses
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving the 2026 property tax rates for municipal, regional, and Business Improvement Area (BIA) purposes. This proposed decision would set the tax rates that property owners, including small business owners who lease or own commercial property, will pay in 2026. The rates cover three parts: the city's portion (municipal), the regional district's portion (regional), and any special BIA levy for businesses in designated improvement areas. Council will consider this at an upcoming meeting — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit comments if you want to weigh in on the proposed tax rates.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Food Service
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.3
2026 PROPERTY TAX RATES
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for 2026 municipal, regional and business improvement area (BIA) property tax rates.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to appoint an Approving Officer
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to appoint an Approving Officer. An Approving Officer is a person who has the authority to approve or reject certain land-related applications, such as subdivision plans. This decision is about who will hold that role, not about changing any rules. The appointment has not been made yet — Council will decide at the upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from you right now. Watch for the council decision if you have a pending subdivision or land application.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.4
APPOINTMENT OF APPROVING OFFICER
Purpose: To seek Council approval to appoint an Approving Officer.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on retaining wall height variance at 8012 11th Ave
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a Development Variance Permit (DVP) for a property at 8012 11th Avenue. A DVP is a special permission that lets a property owner break certain rules in the zoning bylaw. In this case, the owner wants to build retaining walls that are taller than what the current rules allow. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider the request at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own or develop property near 8012 11th Avenue, watch for the council decision — it could set a precedent for similar height variances in your area.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.5
DVP #26-01 - 8012 11TH AVENUE - RETAINING WALLS HEIGHT VARIANCES
Purpose: To seek Council approval for Development Variance Permit (DVP) #26-01.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to award insurance contracts for property and liability programs
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider approving the renewal of contracts for the city's annual property and liability insurance programs. This is a routine business decision to ensure the city has proper insurance coverage for its buildings, vehicles, and operations. The decision does not directly change any rules for businesses, but it affects how the city manages its own risk and financial protection.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.6
2026 INSURANCE PROGRAMS - CONTRACT RENEWALS
Purpose: To request approval to award insurance contracts for the annual property and liability insurance programs.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to approve Heritage Commission trip to 2026 BC conference
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to allow members of Burnaby's Community Heritage Commission (CHC) to attend the 2026 Annual Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC, from May 4-6, 2026. This is a request for approval of travel and attendance, not a final decision yet.

key_dates.label: Heritage BC Conference attendance period (if approved)

agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.7
2026 HERITAGE BC CONFERENCE
Purpose: To seek Council approval for members of the Community Heritage Commission (CHC) to attend the 2026 Annual Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC from May 4-6, 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract award for networking software licenses
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for the supply and delivery of networking software licenses. If approved, this would authorize the city to purchase the software needed to run its computer networks. A 'contract award' means the city would choose a company to provide these licenses and agree to pay them for the work. This decision is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting and has not yet been approved.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.8
CA - NETWORKING SOFTWARE LICENSES
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the supply and delivery of networking software licenses.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract for new civic facility furniture
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for the supply, delivery, and installation of office and facility furniture for a new civic facility. If approved, this contract would cover all the furniture and equipment needed to fit out the new building. The decision is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting and has not yet been finalized.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.9
CA - NEW CIVIC FACILITY FIT-OUT, FURNISHINGS AND EQUIPMENT
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the supply, delivery and installation of office and facility furniture.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract change for external audit services
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract change for the city's external audit services. This means council will decide whether to approve a change to the agreement with the company that audits the city's financial records. If approved, the change would alter the terms of the existing contract. The decision is not final yet — council will discuss and vote on it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.10
CC - EXTERNAL AUDIT SERVICES
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Change (CC) for External Audit Services.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Contract change for geotechnical work at Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed contract change (a formal amendment to an existing agreement) for geotechnical consulting services at the Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex. Geotechnical consultants study soil and ground conditions to ensure buildings are safe and stable. If approved, this change would adjust the scope or cost of the current consulting contract for this recreation complex project.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from you at this time. Watch for the council decision — this could affect the project timeline or costs.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.11
CC - BURNABY LAKE RECREATION COMPLEX GEOTECHNICAL CONSULTING SERVICES
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Change (CC) for geotechnical consultant services for the Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex project.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on Spring 2026 community grant recipients
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving the recommended recipients for the Spring 2026 community grant program. This is a proposed decision — if approved, the grants would provide funding to local community groups and non-profits. The vote will happen at an upcoming council meeting. Business owners who partner with or rely on community organizations should watch for the outcome, as it could affect local programs and services.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.1
ECC - SPRING 2026 COMMUNITY GRANT APPLICATIONS
Purpose: To seek Council approval for the recommended grant recipients for the Spring 2026 intake.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Mid-year 2026 fee changes to Burnaby Fees and Charges Bylaw
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider mid-year 2026 amendments to the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. This proposed bylaw would update certain fees for services like permits, licences, and other city charges. The changes are not yet approved — council will vote on them at an upcoming meeting. If passed, the new fees would take effect later in 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and review the proposed fee changes — they could affect your business licence or permit costs.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.2
FMC - BURNABY CONSOLIDATED FEES AND CHARGES BYLAW - 2026 MID-YEAR AMENDMENTS
Purpose: To propose mid-year 2026 fee amendments to certain fee schedules within the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw and Framework up for Council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed Tenant Protection Framework and a new Tenant Protection Bylaw. If approved, this bylaw would create new rules for landlords, including rules about when and how they can end a tenancy, renovictions, and how much notice they must give tenants. The goal is to give renters more stability and prevent unfair evictions. This is a proposed decision — it has not been passed yet. Council will discuss and vote on it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Landlords and property managers in Burnaby should review the proposed bylaw and attend the council meeting or public hearing to have your say.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.3
PDC - PROPOSED TENANT PROTECTION FRAMEWORK AND TENANT PROTECTION BYLAW AND ASSOCIATED BYLAW AMENDMENTS
Purpose: To seek Council endorsement of the proposed tenant protection framework and Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw and associated bylaw amendments.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on Southeast Bikeway Upgrade improvements
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on proposed improvements for the Southeast Bikeway Upgrade Project. If approved, this project would add new bike lanes, crossings, and safety upgrades along the route. The decision is not final yet — Council will discuss and vote on the plan at an upcoming meeting. Business owners near the bikeway should be aware that construction could affect traffic, parking, and customer access in the area.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, prepare for possible construction impacts on your business access and parking.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.4
TC - SOUTHEAST BIKEWAY UPGRADE PROJECT - PROPOSED IMPROVEMENTS
Purpose: To seek Council approval of the proposed improvements as part of the Southeast Bikeway Upgrade Project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update design rules for safer intersections
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposal to update the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards. The change would require new 'protected intersection' designs that make streets safer for people walking and biking. This is not a final decision yet — Council is set to vote on whether to move forward with the amendment. If approved, future development projects in the Town Centre would need to follow these safer street designs.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this could affect your development or renovation plans in Burnaby's Town Centre.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.5
TC - PROPOSED AMENDMENT TO THE BURNABY TOWN CENTRE DESIGN STANDARDS TO INCORPORATE PROTECTED INTERSECTION GEOMETRY
Purpose: To seek Council endorsement to amend the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards to incorporate protected intersection geometry, improve road safety for pedestrians and cyclists and support all-ages-and-abilities mobility.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on exempting Brentwood Park properties from provincial housing rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a motion that would exempt certain Brentwood Park properties from provincial Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing (SSMUH) requirements. These properties have Restrictive Covenants — legal promises written into the land title that limit what can be built. The motion asks the city to seek an exemption from the province so that these covenants can stay in place, meaning the properties would not have to follow the new provincial rules that allow more housing units on single-family lots. Council will consider this proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, this could affect development options on your Brentwood Park property.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.1
BRENTWOOD PARK SSMUH MOTION - RESTRICTIVE COVENANTS AND STATUTORY BUILDING SCHEMES
Purpose: To respond to Council’s September 9, 2025, motion regarding exempting Brentwood Park properties with Restrictive Covenants from Provincial SSMUH requirements.

attachments.title: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schem

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive building permit activity report for March 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will receive a report on building permits issued in Burnaby from March 1 to March 31, 2026. This is an information-only item — no vote or decision is required. The report gives Council a snapshot of construction activity in the city during that month.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.2
SU: BUILDING PERMIT ACTIVITY REPORT NO. 03 FROM MARCH 1 - MARCH 31, 2026
Purpose: To provide Council with information on construction activity as reflected by the building permits that have been issued for the subject period.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Heritage designation for 3789 Royal Oak Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider an as-found report and Statement of Significance for the heritage resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue. This report describes the property's heritage value and could lead to a future bylaw that protects the building from major changes or demolition. The decision has not been made yet — Council is reviewing the information first.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.3
CHC - AS-FOUND REPORT AND STATEMENT OF SIGNIFICANCE FOR HERITAGE RESOURCES AT 3789 ROYAL OAK AVENUE
Purpose: To provide Council an as-found report and Statement of Significance for heritage resources located at 7389 Royal Oak Avenue for information.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby solid waste and recycling 2025 annual report
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider the City of Burnaby's 2025 annual report on solid waste and recycling collection. This report summarizes how the city's garbage, recycling, and organics collection system performed over the past year. It is a proposed update for council's review and discussion — no new rules or fees have been decided yet.
agenda_briefs.industries: Retail, Food Service, Landlord & Property Mgmt
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.4
EC - SOLID WASTE AND RECYCLING (SWR) 2025 ANNUAL REPORT
Purpose: To provide Council with a summary of the City of Burnaby solid waste and recycling collection system performance.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive update on proposed food sustainability measures in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive an operational update on proposed food sustainability measures. This update will outline current and planned actions to improve food security and reduce waste within the city. No final decisions have been made yet, but the report will inform future council votes on related policies and programs.
agenda_briefs.industries: Food Service, Grocery & Food Retail, Agriculture
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.5
EC - SU: ENHANCING FOOD SUSTAINABILITY IN BURNABY
Purpose: To provide Council with an operational update on food sustainability measures within the City of Burnaby.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Safety upgrades at Central Boulevard and Metropolis parkade intersection
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on proposed safety improvements at the intersection of Central Boulevard and the Metropolis at Metrotown underground parkade access. The proposal includes changes to traffic signals, crosswalks, and signage to make the area safer for drivers, pedestrians, and cyclists. A final decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.6
TC - CENTRAL BOULEVARD AND PARKADE INERSECTION SAFETY IMPROVEMENTS
Purpose: To provide Council with an overview of the proposed safety improvements at the intersection of Central Boulevard and Metropolis at Metrotown underground parkade access.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning amendment for 3900 Grand Promenade, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning amendment for a large development at 3900 Grand Promenade, 6950 Austin Road, and 9858/9898 Gatineau Place. The developer wants to change a previous approval to replace some planned office space with a hotel and restaurant, add 362 more market strata (condo) units, and add extra storeys to two towers. The developer also proposes to move some required affordable housing commitments to a different site (the Carrigan site) for now. This is a proposed change, not yet approved.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing or submit a written comment to Burnaby City Council before the vote.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.1
#14808 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 7, 2026 - REZ #24-23 (3900 Grand Promenade, 6950 Austin Rd., 9858 & 9898 Gatineau Place)
Purpose: to amend the previous rezoning of the subject lands pursuant to REZ #18-32 (Bylaw No. 14447), which received Final Adoption on September 23, 2024, and included two market strata residential buildings of 80 (Tower 1B) and 73 (Tower 1A) storeys, respectively, inclusive of a 13-storey retail and office podium. As part of the subject amendment rezoning application, the applicant is proposing to replace a portion of the commercial office use with hotel and restaurant uses, and add 362 additional market strata units from the development concept approved under REZ #18-32, resulting in an additional seven storeys for Tower 1B and four storeys for Tower 1A. In addition, as part of the subject amendment rezoning application, the applicant is proposing to provide the inclusionary requirement associated with Phase 1 only, to be transferred to the Carrigan site, which is an amendment from the previous rezoning approval.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 12-storey mixed-use development at 6535 Kingsway
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would allow a 12-storey mixed-use building at 6535 Kingsway. The project includes 290 residential units, ground-floor commercial space facing Kingsway, and underground parking. This proposal is consistent with the city's Official Community Plan and the Edmonds Community Plan. If approved, the rezoning would change the current zoning rules to permit this type of development on the site.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing or submit written comments to Burnaby City Council before the vote to share your support or concerns.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.2
#14809 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 8, 2026 - REZ #25-09 (6535 Kingsway)
Purpose: to permit the construction of a 12-storey mixed-use multiple-family development with a double-height commercial storey at street level comprised of 290 residential units, ground-oriented commercial uses fronting Kingsway, and underground parking. The proposal is consistent with the applicable OCP and the Edmonds Community Plan designations for the site.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Five-storey self-storage building at 2757 Norland Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would allow a five-storey enclosed self-storage building at 2757 Norland Avenue. The rezoning application (REZ #25-21) would change the current zoning rules to permit this new building. The proposal follows the city's 1998 Official Community Plan (OCP) — the long-term plan for land use — and approved transition rules. This is not yet approved; Council will decide at a future meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date and attend to have your say on this rezoning proposal.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.3
#14810 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 9, 2026 - REZ #25-21 (2757 Norland Avenue)
Purpose: to permit the construction of a five-storey enclosed self-storage building. The proposal is being advanced in accordance with the applicable 1998 OCP policies and approved transitionary provisions.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby e-bike and e-scooter pilot program bylaw amendment
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to Burnaby's Street and Traffic Bylaw that would create a pilot program for e-bikes and electric kick scooters. If approved, the new rules would allow these devices on certain streets and paths for a trial period. The amendment is called 'Bylaw No. 1, 2026' and would change the existing 1964 traffic bylaw. This is not yet law — it is up for discussion and a vote at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit comments to have your say on the proposed pilot program.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.1
#14811 - Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1964 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby e-bike and e-scooter pilot in parks
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would allow e-bikes and electric kick scooters in certain Burnaby parks as part of a pilot program. The amendment to the Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979 would create new rules for where and how these devices can be used. This is not yet law — the decision is still pending.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this pilot could affect where you can ride e-bikes and e-scooters in Burnaby parks.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.2
#14812 - Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendment to enforce e-bike and e-scooter rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw. This change would allow the city to issue tickets and fines for rules related to e-bikes and electric kick scooters as part of a pilot program. If approved, the city would have the power to enforce things like where these devices can be ridden and how fast they can go. The amendment is needed to support a new pilot program that allows e-bikes and e-scooters on certain city streets and paths.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, new e-bike and e-scooter rules could affect your business operations or deliveries.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.3
#14813 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 3, 2026 (e-bike and electric kick scooter programs)
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 property tax rates for all land and buildings
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would set the 2026 tax rates for all taxable land and buildings in Burnaby. This bylaw, called the Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026, is required under sections 197 and 216 of the Community Charter (the main law that gives cities their powers). If approved, it will determine how much property tax you pay next year based on the assessed value of your land and any improvements (like buildings or renovations).
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.4
#14814 - Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to establish tax rates upon all taxable land and improvements pursuant to sections 197 and 216 of the Community Charter

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, replacing 1965 rules
minutes_briefs.summary: Council voted to repeal the old Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 and adopt the new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, effective July 1, 2026. The new bylaw updates zoning rules, development permit area guidelines, and property zone assignments. It also includes a tenant protection rule: if a tenant eligible for a replacement unit declines their right of first refusal or leaves the unit, that unit must be converted into an inclusionary (affordable) unit. Council also authorized amendments to the Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw and the Waivers and Reductions of Amenity Cost Charges and Development Cost Charges Bylaw. No public hearing was held because the bylaw is consistent with the Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review how the new Zoning Bylaw 2026 affects your property or development plans before the July 1, 2026 effective date.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $2M in Year 2 operating funding for Burnaby Housing Authority
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously approved a $2.0 million transfer to the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation for its second year of operations. The funding, approved on April 28, 2026, will support the Authority's ongoing work to create and manage affordable housing in the city. Council directed city staff to complete the transfer of funds.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
7.2 BHA - ASSISTANCE REQUEST FOR YEAR 2 OPERATING FUNDING
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 191
THAT the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation’s Year 2 Operating Funding assistance request, as outlined in the report titled “BHA – Assistance Request for Year 2 Operating Funding”, dated for April 28, 2026, and Attachment 1, be approved; and
THAT City staff be directed to transfer $2.0 million in Year 2 operating funding to the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 2026 property tax rates for Burnaby businesses
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to approve the 2026 Municipal, Regional and Business Improvement Area (BIA) property tax rates. This means property owners in Burnaby, including businesses located in BIA districts, will pay the new rates for the 2026 tax year. Council also authorized the City Solicitor to prepare and bring forward a formal bylaw (a local law) called the Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 to officially put these rates into effect.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review your 2026 property tax bill to confirm the new rates and budget accordingly for the increased costs.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
minutes_briefs.source_body:
7.3 2026 PROPERTY TAX RATES
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 192
THAT the Municipal, Regional and Business Improvement Area Property Tax Rates for 2026, as outlined in the report titled “2026 Property Tax Rates” dated April 28, 2026, be approved; and,
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward a Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026, to implement the Municipal, Regional and Business Improvement Area Property Tax Rates for 2026.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Mark Norton as Burnaby's Approving Officer under Land Title Act
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved the appointment of Mark Norton as an Approving Officer for the City of Burnaby. This appointment is made under Section 77 of the Land Title Act, which gives the officer the authority to approve or reject plans for land subdivision and development within the city. The decision is now final.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
7.4 APPOINTMENT OF APPROVING OFFICER
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 193
THAT Mark Norton be appointed as an Approving Officer for the City of Burnaby in accordance with Section 77 of the Land Title Act.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved retaining wall height variances at 8012 11th Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved Development Variance Permit #26-01 for the property at 8012 11th Avenue. This permit allows the owner to build retaining walls taller than what the current zoning bylaw normally allows. A development variance permit is a special permission that lets a property owner deviate from specific zoning rules. The City Solicitor has been directed to register this permit with the Land Title Office, making it official on the property's title.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own 8012 11th Avenue, ensure your retaining wall construction matches the approved variances in the permit.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
7.5 DVP #26-01 - 8012 11TH AVENUE - RETAINING WALLS HEIGHT VARIANCES
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 194
THAT the issuance of Development Variance Permit #26-01, included as Attachment 2 in the report titled “DVP #26-01 – 8012 11th Avenue – Retaining Wall Height Variances” dated April 28, 2026, be approved; and
THAT the City Solicitor be directed to register notice of DVP #26-01 with the Land Title Office.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $4,013,855 for 2026 insurance contract renewals with Aon Reed Stenhouse
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved spending $4,013,855 to renew twelve insurance contracts with Aon Reed Stenhouse Inc. for 2026. This covers the city's insurance premiums for the coming year. The decision was made at the April 28, 2026 council meeting and is now final.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
7.6 2026 INSURANCE PROGRAMS - CONTRACT RENEWALS
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 195
THAT the total amount of $4,013,855 payable to Aon Reed Stenhouse Inc. for insurance premiums for all twelve insurance contracts, as outlined in the report titled “2026 Insurance Program – Contract Renewals” dated for April 28, 2026, be approved.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $4,000 for Heritage Commission members to attend conference
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved spending up to $4,000 to send up to two members of the Community Heritage Commission to the 2026 Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC, from May 4-6, 2026. The decision was carried unanimously. This allows commission members to attend the conference for professional development and networking related to heritage conservation.

key_dates.label: Heritage BC Conference in Squamish takes place

minutes_briefs.source_body:
7.7 2026 HERITAGE BC CONFERENCE
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 196
THAT an expenditure of up to $4,000 for up to two (2) Community Heritage Commission members to attend the 2026 Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC from May 4-6, 2026, be approved.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $1,047,545 Cisco software license contract to Insight Canada Inc.
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved a contract award to Insight Canada Inc. for Cisco DNA networking software licenses at an estimated cost of $1,047,545 including taxes. The final payment will be based on the actual quantity of goods and services delivered. This decision was made at the council meeting and is now complete.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
7.8 CA - NETWORKING SOFTWARE LICENSES
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 197
THAT a contract award to Insight Canada Inc. for an estimated cost of $1,047,545 including taxes, as outlined in the report titled “CA – Networking Software Licenses” dated April 28, 2026, be approved; and
THAT final payment will be based on the actual quantity of goods and services delivered.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $1.96M furniture contract for new civic facility fit-out
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved a contract award to Heritage Office Furnishings Ltd. for $1,962,600 (including taxes) to supply furniture, furnishings, and equipment for the new civic facility fit-out. Council also approved a contingency of $196,260 (including tax) to cover unexpected costs. Final payment will be based on the actual quantity of goods and services delivered.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
7.9 CA - NEW CIVIC FACILITY FIT-OUT, FURNISHINGS AND EQUIPMENT
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 198
THAT a contract award to Heritage Office Furnishings Ltd. for an estimated cost of $1,962,600 including taxes, as outlined in the report titled “CA – New Civic Facility Fit-Out, Furnishings and Equipment” dated April 28, 2026, be approved;
THAT a contingency for an estimated amount of $196,260 including tax be approved; and
THAT final payment will be based on the actual quantity of goods and services delivered.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $47,000 contract change for KPMG audit services
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved a $47,000 contract change to KPMG LLP for external audit services. The final payment will be based on actual hours worked and services delivered, not a fixed amount. This decision was made at the April 28, 2026 council meeting.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
7.10 CC - EXTERNAL AUDIT SERVICES
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 199
THAT a contract change to KPMG LLP for an estimated cost of $47,000 including taxes, as outlined in the report titled “CC - External Audit Services” dated April 28, 2026, be approved; and
THAT final payment will be based on actual quantity of services delivered and hourly rates as submitted.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $384,893 contract change for Burnaby Lake geotechnical work
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved a $384,893 contract change to WSP Canada Inc. for geotechnical consulting services at the Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex. The change covers additional soil and ground testing needed for the project. Council also approved a $73,500 contingency fund for unexpected costs. Final payment will be based on actual work done and hourly rates. The decision was unanimous.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
7.11 CC - BURNABY LAKE RECREATION COMPLEX GEOTECHNICAL CONSULTING SERVICES
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 200
THAT a contract change to WSP Canada Inc. for an estimated cost of $384,893 including taxes, as outlined in the report titled “CC – Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex Geotechnical Consulting Services” dated April 28, 2026, be approved;
THAT a contingency for an estimated amount of $73,500 including tax be approved; and
THAT final payment will be based on the actual quantity of goods and services delivered and hourly rates submitted.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $175,000 in Spring 2026 community grants for Burnaby groups
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council unanimously approved 31 community grants for Spring 2026, totaling over $175,000. The grants cover five categories: Recreation Sports (RS), Operating (O), Initiative (I), Subsidized Use (SU) of City facilities, and Food Security (FS). Recipients include the Burnaby Female Ice Hockey Association, the Society to End Homelessness in Burnaby, MOSAIC BC, and several food security organizations. The grants support local sports teams, community events, newcomer services, and food programs.

key_dates.label: Burnaby community grant programs — application window for Fall 2026 cycle opens

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved mid-year fee changes for Burnaby parks, rec, and culture programs.
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council unanimously approved mid-year amendments to the Parks, Recreation and Culture fees under the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. The changes update Schedule F1 (effective June 1, 2026) and replace Schedule F3 (effective September 1, 2026). Council also lowered the eligibility age for the Seniors Lifetime Card from 90 to 80 years old. These fee updates affect program registration, facility rentals, and membership costs for residents and businesses.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the updated fee schedules (Attachments 1 and 2) to see how your program or facility rental costs will change starting June 1 and September 1, 2026.

key_dates.label: Schedule F1 fee amendments take effect — Burnaby Parks, Rec & Culture
Schedule F3 fee amendments take effect — Burnaby Parks, Rec & Culture

minutes_briefs.source_body:
8.2 FMC - BURNABY CONSOLIDATED FEES AND CHARGES BYLAW - 2026 MID-YEAR AMENDMENTS
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 206
THAT the proposed Parks, Recreation and Culture fee updates, as described in Sections 4.1 and 4.2, and as outlined in Attachments 1 and 2, of the report titled “Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw – 2026 Mid-Year Amendments” dated April 21, 2026, of the Financial Management Committee meeting, be approved;
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward a bylaw to amend the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, to amend Schedule F1 as described in Section 4.1 of the report, and substantially as set out in Attachment 1 to the report, effective June 1, 2026; and
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward a bylaw to amend the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, to repeal and replace Schedule F3, substantially as set out in Attachment 2 to the report, effective September 1, 2026; and
THAT the Seniors Lifetime Card membership eligibility, as outlined on page 6 of Attachment 2, be lowered from 90 years and older to 80 years and older.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby Council approved Tenant Protection Bylaw with inclusionary unit amendment
minutes_briefs.summary: Council voted to approve the Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw, with a key change: if a tenant declines their right of first refusal or vacates a replacement unit, that unit must become an inclusionary (affordable) unit. The bylaw gives tenants a right of first refusal to return after redevelopment and ensures fair treatment. The City Solicitor was authorized to finalize the bylaw and related amendments to the Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw. Staff will report back during biannual reviews on how to keep tenant protections strong while maximizing affordable housing.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the new Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw to understand how it affects your rental properties and tenant rights.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant, Construction & Development
minutes_briefs.source_body:
8.3 PDC - PROPOSED TENANT PROTECTION FRAMEWORK AND TENANT PROTECTION BYLAW AND ASSOCIATED BYLAW AMENDMENTS
...
MOVED AND SECONDED
Resolution No. 2026 – 189
THAT the proposed framework for tenant protection described in the report titled “Proposed Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and Associated Bylaw Amendments,” dated March 31, 2026, of the Planning and Development Committee meeting, with the exception that all replacement units be converted into inclusionary units should an eligible tenant decline their right of first refusal or vacate a replacement unit, be approved;
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026, in the form substantially set out in Attachment 1 to the report;
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward amendments to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, substantially as set out in Attachment 3 to the report; and
THAT the application of the Tenant Assistance Policy and Rental Use Zoning Policy following the effective date of the Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw, as set out in section 5.0 of the report, be approved; and,
THAT staff report back to Council during the biannual review of the Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw and Inclusionary Zoning through the Rental Use Zoning Policy, options to ensure that the principles of tenant protection (a right-of-first-refusal for returning tenants and a fair and equitable treatment of all returning and future tenants) are preserved while maximizing the impact of affordable housing on Burnaby residents
CARRIED
(Opposed: Councillors Calendino, Dhaliwal and Lee)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Southeast Bikeway Upgrade and advanced it to design
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has unanimously approved the proposed improvements for the Southeast Bikeway Upgrade Project. The project will now move forward to the detailed design stage. This means the city will begin planning the specific layout and construction details for the bikeway improvements. Business owners along the route should be aware that construction may follow once the design is complete, but no construction timeline has been set yet.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required. Monitor city communications for future construction updates that may affect access to your business.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
8.4 TC - SOUTHEAST BIKEWAY UPGRADE PROJECT - PROPOSED IMPROVEMENTS
...
Resolution No. 2026 - 207
THAT the proposed improvements, as outlined in the report titled "Southeast Bikeway Upgrade Project - Proposed Improvements" dated April 8, 2026, of the Transportation Committee meeting, be approved; and
THAT the proposed improvements, as outlined in this report, be advanced to detailed design.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council referred intersection design changes back to staff for more review
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to send proposed changes to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards back to staff for further review. The changes would add protected intersection geometry — a safer design for crosswalks and bike lanes at busy corners. Staff will study the proposal and report back to Council at a later date. No new rules are in effect yet.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
8.5 TC - PROPOSED AMENDMENT TO THE BURNABY TOWN CENTRE DESIGN STANDARDS TO INCORPORATE PROTECTED INTERSECTION GEOMETRY
...
MOVED AND SECONDED
Resolution No. 2026 – 208
THAT the proposed amendments to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards, as outlined in the discussion section of the proposed motion titled "Proposed Amendment to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards to Incorporate Protected Intersection Geometry", dated April 8, 2026, of the Transportation Committee meeting, be REFERRED to staff for further review and report back to Council.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council defeated motion to amend Building Bylaw for Brentwood Park covenants
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted down a motion that would have directed staff to amend the Building Bylaw to address restrictive covenants and statutory building schemes in the Brentwood Park area. The motion was defeated on a vote of 5 opposed (Councillors Calendino, Gu, Santiago, Tetrault and Wang). This means the Building Bylaw will not be changed as proposed, and existing restrictive covenants — legal promises written into property deeds that limit how land can be used — will remain in place for now.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
9.1 BRENTWOOD PARK SSMUH MOTION - RESTRICTIVE COVENANTS AND STATUTORY BUILDING SCHEMES
...
Arising from discussion, Mayor Hurley introduced the following motion:
MOVED AND SECONDED
THAT staff be directed to undertake the amendments to the Building Bylaw, as outlined in the report titled "Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes" dated April 28, 2026.
DEFEATED
(Opposed: Councillors Calendino, Gu, Santiago, Tetrault and Wang)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved first two readings for Grand Promenade rezoning
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted to give first and second reading to Bylaw 14808, which would rezone properties at 3900 Grand Promenade, 6950 Austin Road, and 9858 & 9898 Gatineau Place. This is an early step in the rezoning process. The bylaw still needs a public hearing and third reading before it becomes final. Councillor Dhaliwal opposed the motion.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Monitor for the public hearing date if your business is in this area — you may want to provide input.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
11.1.1 #14808 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 7, 2026 - REZ #24-23 (3900 Grand Promenade, 6950 Austin Rd., 9858 & 9898 Gatineau Place)
MOVED AND SECONDED
Resolution No. 2026 - 216
THAT Bylaw No. 14808 be now read a first and second time.
CARRIED
(Opposed: Councillor Dhaliwal)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved first and second reading for rezoning at 6535 Kingsway
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to give first and second reading to Bylaw 14809, which would rezone the property at 6535 Kingsway. This is a step in the process to change the zoning rules for that land. The bylaw still needs third reading and adoption before it takes effect.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required. Monitor for third reading and public hearing dates if you own or plan to develop this property.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
11.1.2 #14809 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 8, 2026 - REZ #25-09 (6535 Kingsway)
MOVED AND SECONDED

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved first two readings for rezoning at 2757 Norland Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted unanimously to give first and second reading to Bylaw 14810, which would rezone the property at 2757 Norland Avenue. This is an early step in the rezoning process. The bylaw still needs third reading and adoption before it takes effect.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required. Monitor for the public hearing date if you own or plan to develop this property.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
11.1.3 #14810 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 9, 2026 - REZ #25-21 (2757 Norland Avenue)
MOVED AND SECONDED

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved amendments to Burnaby's Street and Traffic Bylaw
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has unanimously approved amendments to the Street and Traffic Bylaw (Bylaw 14811). This bylaw governs rules for streets, sidewalks, and traffic within the city. The amendment was given first, second, and third reading, meaning it has been formally passed and is now law. Business owners should be aware that any changes to parking, loading zones, or street use rules under this bylaw are now in effect.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the updated Street and Traffic Bylaw to ensure your business operations comply with any new parking, loading, or street-use rules.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
11.2.1 #14811 - Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
MOVED AND SECONDED
Resolution No. 2026 - 217-1
THAT Bylaw No. 14811 be now read a first, second and third time.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved updates to Burnaby's Parks Regulation Bylaw
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has unanimously approved an amendment to the Parks Regulation Bylaw (Bylaw 14812). The bylaw was given first, second, and third reading, meaning it has been formally adopted and is now law. This update changes the rules for how parks in Burnaby are used and regulated.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review the updated Parks Regulation Bylaw to ensure your park-related activities or events comply with the new rules now in effect.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
11.2.2 #14812 - Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
MOVED AND SECONDED
Resolution No. 2026 - 217-2
THAT Bylaw No. 14812 be now read a first, second and third time.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Bylaw Notice Enforcement amendment (Bylaw 14813)
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has unanimously passed an amendment to the Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw (Bylaw 14813). This means the rules for how the city issues and enforces tickets for bylaw violations (like property standards or noise complaints) have been updated. The bylaw was given all three required readings and is now law.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
minutes_briefs.source_body:
11.2.3 #14813 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 3, 2026
MOVED AND SECONDED
Resolution No. 2026 - 217-3
THAT Bylaw No. 14813 be now read a first, second and third time.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 setting property tax rates
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has unanimously adopted the Rates Bylaw 2026 (Bylaw 14814). This bylaw sets the property tax rates that businesses and homeowners in Burnaby will pay for the 2026 tax year. The bylaw was given first, second, and third reading in a single vote, meaning it has been formally approved and is now law. Business owners should expect their 2026 property tax bills to reflect the rates set in this bylaw.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review your 2026 property tax bill when issued to confirm the new rates and budget accordingly.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
minutes_briefs.source_body:
11.2.4 #14814 - Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026
MOVED AND SECONDED
Resolution No. 2026 - 217-4
THAT Bylaw No. 14814 be now read a first, second and third time.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved re-nominations of Mayors Lahti and Johnstone to E-Comm Board
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved the re-nomination of Mayor Lahti from Port Moody and the nomination of Mayor Johnstone from New Westminster to the E-Comm Board of Directors for the 2026-2027 term. E-Comm is the emergency communications centre that provides 9-1-1 and dispatch services for the region. The decision was carried unanimously and will be shared with neighbouring cities and E-Comm.
minutes_briefs.locations: Burnaby, New Westminster, Coquitlam, Port Coquitlam, Port Moody
minutes_briefs.source_body:
14. RELEASE OF CLOSED MEETING DECISIONS
Resolution No. C2026 - 045
THAT the re-nomination of Mayor Lahti, City of Port Moody, and the nomination of Mayor Johnstone, City of New Westminster, to the E-Comm Board of Directors for the 2026-2027 term be approved; and
THAT this resolution be released for public information and forwarded to the City of Port Moody, City of Coquitlam, City of New Westminster, City of Port Coquitlam, Village of Belcarra, and E-Comm.
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY
